title: Chapter 11 Quiz: Sessions & Cookies
description: Test your understanding of sessions and cookies!

questions.question: What is the main reason to use sessions?
options: To maintain state across HTTP requests
questions.explanation: Sessions allow the server to remember users across requests, overcoming HTTP statelessness.

questions.question: session_start() must be called before any output.
questions.explanation: Yes, because it sends a cookie header.

questions.question: Where is session data stored on the server by default?
questions.explanation: Default session storage is files in the system's temp directory.

questions.question: How do you store a value 'user_id' in the session?
questions.explanation: Use the $_SESSION superglobal array.

questions.question: Cookies are stored on the server.
questions.explanation: Cookies are stored on the client's browser.

questions.question: Which function is used to set a cookie?
questions.explanation: setcookie() is the correct function.

questions.question: What does the 'httponly' flag do?
options: Makes cookie only sent over HTTPS
Prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie
Makes cookie expire after session ends
Restricts cookie to a specific path
questions.explanation: httponly prevents access via JavaScript, mitigating XSS.

questions.question: To delete a cookie, set its expiration time to a value in the ______.
questions.explanation: Setting expiration in the past makes the browser discard it.

questions.question: Session variables are accessible via $_SESSION after session_start().
questions.explanation: Yes, session_start() populates $_SESSION.

questions.question: What happens if you don't set an expiration time for a cookie?
options: It becomes a session cookie and expires when the browser closes
questions.explanation: Without expiration, the cookie is a session cookie.

questions.question: Which superglobal contains cookie data?
questions.explanation: $_COOKIE holds cookies sent by the browser.

questions.question: session_destroy() immediately unsets all session variables.
questions.explanation: session_destroy() destroys the session data on server, but $_SESSION may still be populated in the current request. You should also clear $_SESSION.

questions.question: What is the default lifetime of a session?
questions.explanation: Session lifetime is configured in php.ini (session.gc_maxlifetime), typically 24 minutes.

questions.question: Which PHP function regenerates the session ID to prevent session fixation?
questions.explanation: session_regenerate_id() creates a new session ID while preserving data.

questions.question: Cookies can store up to 4KB of data typically.
questions.explanation: Yes, most browsers limit cookie size to around 4KB.

questions.question: How do you unset a single session variable?
questions.explanation: unset($_SESSION['var']) removes that key. Setting to null leaves the key with null value.

questions.question: What is a common security risk when using cookies?
questions.explanation: All are risks mitigated by secure flags, httponly, and samesite.

questions.question: The 'samesite' cookie attribute helps prevent CSRF attacks.
questions.explanation: Yes, it restricts how cookies are sent with cross-site requests.

questions.question: What does header('Location: page.php') do?
questions.explanation: It sends a redirect header.

questions.question: In a login system, why should you use sessions instead of cookies to store logged-in status?
options: Session data is stored on the server, not exposed to client
questions.explanation: Storing logged-in status in a cookie would allow users to tamper with it; sessions keep data on the server.
